title: Facial recognition misidentification contributes to wrongful arrest of innocent person
date_note: Date of the wrongful arrest as reported in ACLU documentation and subsequent civil proceedings. The facial recognition match that contributed to the arrest was generated by the Detroit Police Department's automated facial recognition system. The individual was released after approximately 30 hours of detention.
ai_system_context: Automated facial recognition matching system used by a US municipal police department to identify a suspect from a low-quality surveillance image. The system returned a match that was subsequently used as a basis for arrest without adequate independent corroboration.
summary: According to documentation published by the American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU) and subsequently reported in multiple outlets, in January 2020 the Detroit Police Department arrested an individual on suspicion of a crime based substantially on a match generated by an automated facial recognition system. The individual, who had no connection to the alleged crime, was detained for approximately 30 hours before being released. The ACLU subsequently published documentation of the case and filed legal action on behalf of the individual. The case is consistent with findings documented in the US National Institute of Standards and Technology Face Recognition Vendor Test (NIST FRVT) reports, which identified statistically higher false positive match rates for individuals with darker skin tones across multiple commercial facial recognition systems. The Detroit Police Department subsequently imposed restrictions on the use of facial recognition for arrest decisions. The case is widely cited in AI governance and civil liberties literature as an example of algorithmic bias contributing to a serious harm in a high-stakes law enforcement context.
evidence_required: EV-012 — Bias testing record: evidence of pre-deployment demographic parity testing for false positive rates across skin tone groups
EV-004 — Human oversight record: documentation of the corroboration steps required between facial recognition match and arrest decision
EV-002 — Risk assessment: pre-deployment risk classification for facial recognition use in law enforcement arrest decisions
EV-013 — Incident log: police department records of the arrest decision and the role of the facial recognition match
lessons: Law enforcement use of automated facial recognition matching must require mandatory independent human corroboration before any arrest decision; a facial recognition match alone should not be sufficient basis for arrest.

Facial recognition systems deployed in high-stakes law enforcement contexts must undergo independent bias testing across demographic groups — including skin tone and gender — before deployment, and results must be documented.
Pre-deployment risk assessments for facial recognition systems in law enforcement must explicitly account for the consequences of false positive matches, including wrongful arrest and detention.
Organisations deploying AI-assisted identification systems should maintain clear documentation of the decision pathway between automated output and consequential human action.

sources.title: ACLU — Robert Williams v. City of Detroit — ACLU lawsuit documentation and case summary, 2021
NIST FRVT Part 3: Demographic Effects — Face Recognition Vendor Test, Grother et al., November 2019 (primary official source on demographic accuracy differentials)

CourtListener/RECAP — Williams v. City of Detroit, U.S. District Court E.D. Michigan — public court docket record for the civil rights action arising from the wrongful arrest (public domain court record)

harms: Wrongful arrest and detention of an innocent person for approximately 30 hours
Psychological harm and reputational impact on the individual and their family
Civil rights violation resulting in ACLU civil action
affected_stakeholders: Individuals subject to facial recognition-assisted arrest decisions
Law enforcement agencies using automated facial recognition systems
Civil liberties bodies and regulatory oversight authorities
impact: An innocent individual was wrongfully arrested and detained for approximately 30 hours. The ACLU filed a civil rights action on behalf of the individual. The Detroit Police Department subsequently restricted use of facial recognition for arrest purposes. The case contributed to legislative and regulatory attention on facial recognition use in law enforcement and is widely cited in AI bias and governance literature.
